1: 'Again I lifted my eyes and saw, and behold, a flying scroll!' The sixth night vision: a flying scroll (megillah ufah) — a large written scroll in motion through the air. Unlike the previous visions, this one is not a static scene but a dynamic object moving through the landscape. The scroll is enormous: twenty by ten cubits (about 9x4.5 meters), the same dimensions as the porch of Solomon's temple (1 Kings 6:3) and the tabernacle's holy place. This dimensional correlation suggests the scroll is the size of the divine dwelling — it is the covenantal content of that dwelling made visible.
2: 'And he said to me, What do you see? I answered, I see a flying scroll. Its length is twenty cubits, and its width ten cubits.' The dimensions confirmed: twenty cubits by ten. The interpreting angel confirms what Zechariah sees before providing the interpretation. The scale of the scroll — impossible for any single person to write or read — indicates its divine origin and the comprehensive scope of its judgments.
3: 'Then he said to me, This is the curse that goes out over the face of the whole land. For everyone who steals shall be cleaned out according to what is on one side, and everyone who swears falsely shall be cleaned out according to what is on the other side.' The two sides of the scroll contain two curses: against stealing (eighth commandment, Exodus 20:15) and against false swearing (third commandment, Exodus 20:7; ninth commandment, Exodus 20:16). These two represent the whole Decalogue — offenses against neighbor (stealing) and against YHWH (swearing falsely by his name). The post-exilic community's internal crimes are targeted: economic exploitation and fraudulent oath-breaking.
4: 'I will send it out, declares YHWH of hosts, and it shall enter the house of the thief, and the house of him who swears falsely by my name. And it shall remain in his house and consume it, both timber and stones.' The scroll enters the criminal's house and consumes it — the covenant curse materializing as architectural destruction. Both timber and stones (veet atzav veet avanav) — the complete structure, leaving nothing. This is the leprosy-in-houses imagery of Leviticus 14:34-45 (a house infected with leprous mold that must be destroyed) raised to a covenantal level: the house of the oath-breaker becomes as ritually unclean as a leprous house.
5: 'Then the angel who talked with me came forward and said to me, Lift your eyes and see what this is that is going out.' The seventh night vision begins: a different kind of vision, more allegorical. The interpreting angel draws Zechariah's attention to something departing ('going out') — the movement from Judah into the wider world.
6: 'And I said, What is it? He said, This is the basket that is going out. And he said, This is their iniquity in all the land.' The basket (efah) — the standard unit of dry measure for grain (cf. Amos 8:5 where it is used for fraudulent measuring). The basket contains the iniquity of the land — the accumulated sin of the community being collected and removed. The efah as the container for iniquity connects to the efah as the site of commercial fraud that the prophets condemned: the instrument of injustice becomes the container for the collected injustice.
7: 'And behold, the leaden cover was lifted, and there was a woman sitting in the basket!' A woman sitting inside the efah — the feminine personification of wickedness. The lead cover (kikar oferet, a disk of lead) is the lid that seals her inside. Lead is the heaviest common metal — impossible to lift without great effort. The vision combines commercial imagery (the grain measure) with feminine personification (wickedness as a woman — a convention in Hebrew wisdom: Prov 5-7, Zechariah 9:13-18).
8: 'And he said, This is Wickedness (harish'ah). And he thrust her back into the basket, and thrust down the lead weight over its opening.' The woman is named: Wickedness (the definite article: this specific wickedness). The angel forcibly pushes her back and reseals the lead lid — the wickedness is captured and restrained. The restraint of evil is a divine act requiring angelic effort; the evil does not go quietly.
9: 'Then I lifted my eyes and saw, and behold, two women coming forward! The wind was in their wings. They had wings like the wings of a stork, and they lifted up the basket between earth and heaven.' Two women with stork wings (kanfei chasidah) carry the efah basket. The stork (chasidah, whose name derives from chesed, loyalty) is an unclean bird (Leviticus 11:19; Deuteronomy 14:18) — evoking the unclean nature of what they carry. They lift the basket between earth and heaven: the wickedness of the land is being transported out of the holy land to another destination.
10: 'Then I said to the angel who talked with me, Where are they taking the basket? He said to me, To the land of Shinar, to build a house for it. And when this is ready, the basket will be set down there on its base.' Shinar = Babylon (Genesis 10:10; Zechariah 11:2; Zechariah 14:1; Daniel 1:2). The house built for wickedness in Babylon — a temple for the personified sin being exported from Judah. The installation of wickedness in Babylon on its proper base (mekunatah — a cultic base for a divine image) ironically confirms Babylon as the proper homeland of what Judah has been exporting. The vision promises the purification of Judah by the export of its wickedness to its land of origin.
11: This verse continues the description of the transport to Shinar (covered in Zechariah 5:10's commentary above).
